html:
Why Roof Drains Cause More Damage Than Almost Any Other Component

A roof drain is a small piece of hardware compared to the membrane around it, but it causes a disproportionate share of the leaks and structural concerns we get called about. A clogged or undersized drain doesn't just mean a puddle — on a flat commercial roof, it means water accumulating faster than the roof can shed it, adding weight the structure wasn't necessarily designed to hold indefinitely.

How a Clogged Drain Turns Into a Structural Problem

Debris — leaves, cottonwood seed, roofing granules that have washed off an aging membrane — collects at drain bowls faster than most owners expect, especially on buildings near mature trees or in areas that catch wind-blown debris from surrounding lots. A clogged drain during a heavy spring rain event can leave several inches of standing water across a roof section within hours. That's more than a membrane concern; enough accumulated water adds real load to a structure, and repeated ponding cycles accelerate membrane aging at a rate that outpaces the rest of the roof.

We've measured ponded sections that held enough water after a single storm to visibly deflect the deck between structural members. That kind of load isn't dramatic in the moment, but a roof that ponds the same way after every heavy rain is carrying that added weight repeatedly, which is a different risk than a single isolated event.

Internal Drains vs. Scuppers: Different Failure Modes

Internal drains route water down through the building's plumbing system and fail primarily through clogging or a deteriorated clamping ring that lets water track underneath the membrane instead of through the drain. Scuppers — openings through a parapet wall that let water exit at roof edge — fail differently, usually through corrosion of the scupper liner or debris blocking the opening from the roof side. A building with both needs both checked, since a scupper backup can mask whether internal drains are also struggling.

We test flow at every drain and scupper during an inspection rather than assuming an opening that looks clear is actually passing water at capacity. A partially blocked line downstream of the roof can look fine at the surface while still backing water up during a heavy rain.

Ponding Water and What It Does to a Membrane Over Time

Standing water that clears within 48 hours after rain is generally considered normal on a low-slope roof. Water that sits longer than that accelerates UV-related aging unevenly across the membrane, promotes algae and debris buildup that further restricts drainage, and in cold months can freeze and thaw repeatedly, stressing seams in the ponded area more than the rest of the field. Chronic ponding in the same location almost always points to either a low spot in the roof's slope or a drain that isn't functioning at capacity.

  • Drain bowl and strainer clearing with a flow test rather than a visual check alone
  • Clamping ring inspection to confirm the drain seal is intact
  • Scupper liner condition and blockage check where scuppers are present
  • Ponding survey identifying areas that hold water beyond 48 hours
  • Drain capacity check against roof square footage and local rainfall intensity

Drainage Corrections Missing From Reroof Scopes

A new membrane installed over a roof that already had a ponding problem will pond in the same spots, because a reroof doesn't fix slope on its own unless tapered insulation or drain relocation is specifically included in the scope. We've seen reroof bids that skip this entirely — new membrane, same low spots, same ponding a year later. If ponding is a known issue, ask directly whether the quote includes a drainage correction or just a new membrane over the same slope.

Sizing Drainage for Missouri Rain Intensity

Rain events in this region can deliver a heavy rate of rainfall in a short window during spring storm systems, and drainage sized for a lighter, steadier rain pattern can be overwhelmed during those bursts even if it handles normal weather fine. We size or verify drain and scupper capacity against the roof's actual square footage and the rainfall intensity this region sees, not a generic standard that might undersize the system here.

This shows up most on additions and expansions where new roof area was tied into an existing drain system without checking whether that system was ever sized to handle the added square footage. The original drains may have been adequate for the original building footprint and undersized the moment the roof grew.

Roof Drainage Questions

How often should roof drains be cleared?

At minimum during spring and fall maintenance visits, with additional checks after major storms or if the building sits near trees that shed debris onto the roof.

Is standing water on a flat roof always a problem?

Water that clears within about 48 hours is generally normal. Water sitting longer than that usually points to a drainage or slope issue worth investigating.

Can a ponding problem be fixed without a full reroof?

Sometimes, through tapered insulation crickets added to redirect water toward existing drains, though severe or widespread ponding may require a fuller correction.

What's the difference between a drain and a scupper?

A drain routes water down through the building's plumbing; a scupper is an opening through a parapet wall that lets water exit at roof edge, often into an exterior gutter or downspout.

Does a clogged drain actually risk structural damage?

Enough accumulated standing water adds real weight to the roof structure, so a severely clogged drain during a heavy rain event is more than a cosmetic problem.
